explanation: The sentence says: In both prop_0 and prop_1, prop_2 must occur if prop_3 holds. Restated, whenever both prop_0 and prop_1 are true, and prop_3 is true, then prop_2 must also be true at that position. In LTL, this is written as: globally if (prop_0 and prop_1 and prop_3) then prop_2.

The requirement specifies that, in certain modes (PCV and PSV), it should be possible for prop_0 to occur, provided that prop_1 is true. This can be interpreted as: if prop_1 is true, then eventually prop_0 can be achieved (i.e., it is possible for prop_0 to become true when prop_1 holds). In LTL, possibility corresponds to the 'eventually' (F) operator. Thus, whenever prop_1 is true, it must be possible (eventually) for prop_0 to become true.

The requirement says: 'In prop_0 and prop_1, there shall be the possibility to prop_2 if it is prop_3.' This means: whenever prop_0 and prop_1 are true, and if prop_3 is also true, then there must be a possibility (i.e., eventually possible) that prop_2 becomes true. In LTL, 'possibility' is captured by the 'F' (eventually) operator. So, whenever (prop_0 & prop_1 & prop_3) holds, it must be possible at some point in the future that prop_2 holds: G((prop_0 & prop_1 & prop_3) -> F(prop_2)).

The requirement states that in PCV and PSV modes, prop_0 should be possible (eventually true) if prop_1 is true. In LTL, this can be encoded as: "Globally, if prop_1 holds, then eventually prop_0 should hold." The formula is: G (prop_1 -> F prop_0)

The requirement states that in the context of PCV and PSV modes (these mode constraints are not specified as atomic propositions, so we will focus on the prop_0 and prop_1 requirements), it must be possible for prop_0 to happen if prop_1 is true. In LTL, saying 'there shall be the possibility to prop_0 if it is prop_1' means that whenever prop_1 happens, it's possible that prop_0 happens as well (i.e., there is no path where prop_1 is true and prop_0 never happens). This can be captured by the formula: globally, if prop_1 is true, then eventually prop_0 will become true.

This requirement says that in PCV and PSV modes (which are assumed to be 'always true' context or handled by another proposition), there should be the possibility (i.e., it is possible at some future time) for prop_0 to happen if prop_1 holds. In LTL, 'possible' is represented as 'eventually' (F), so if prop_1 occurs, then eventually prop_0 must also occur.

The sentence expresses that, within the context of PCV and PSV modes (which must be encoded in context or as atoms not provided here), it should always be possible to achieve prop_0 if prop_1 is true. In LTL, 'possibility' is usually expressed with the 'eventually' (F) operator, so if prop_1 holds, then prop_0 should eventually hold. This is captured by 'G (prop_1 -> F prop_0)'.

The statement says that IF prop_1 holds, THEN it shall be possible (eventually) for prop_0 to occur. This means that whenever prop_1 is true, at some (possibly next or future) point, prop_0 should become true. In LTL, this is captured as: globally, if prop_1 holds, then eventually prop_0 will hold.

The sentence suggests that whenever both 'PCV' and 'prop_0' hold, and if 'prop_2' also holds, then 'prop_1' must also be true at that instant. Since we're not given an atomic proposition for 'PCV', I'll assume you mean the formula triggers when 'prop_0' and 'prop_2' hold. So, globally, whenever 'prop_0 & prop_2' is true, 'prop_1' should also be true.

The statement 'In PCV and PSV modes there shall be the prop_0 if it is prop_1.' translates to: whenever prop_1 holds, prop_0 must also hold. This is a safety property, typically written as 'globally, if prop_1 then prop_0.'
